We observe 10 distinct lines. From shortest to longest wavelength: a pair of violet lines, a single blue-violet line, a single blue-cyan line, a single green-blue line, a strong single green line, a pair of yellow lines, a fainter orange-red line, and a single red line. This pattern of multiple lines across violet, blue, green, yellow, and red, with a prominent green line and a yellow doublet, is characteristic of a Mercury emission spectrum.
No flammable gases or combustion byproducts detected

There are 4 distinct emission lines. From left to right (shortest to longest wavelength): a violet line, followed by a dark blue line very close to it; then a cyan line with a moderate gap; and finally, a red line with a larger gap from the cyan line.

The spectrum in image 6 features a prominent continuous rainbow band in its central region, smoothly transitioning from red through orange, yellow, green, and blue to violet. This central band exhibits no discrete emission lines.
To the far left of the continuous band, there is a single faint discrete emission line observed in the violet-blue region (shorter wavelength).
To the far right of the continuous band, there is a single distinct discrete emission line observed in the yellow-orange region (longer wavelength).

Observed 4 distinct emission lines. From left to right: a prominent violet line, a fainter dark blue/indigo line, a distinct cyan/light blue line, and a distinct red line. The pattern, number, and colors of these lines perfectly match the reference spectrum for Hydrogen.

Observed 4 distinct emission lines. From left to right: a prominent violet line, a fainter dark blue/indigo line, a distinct cyan/light blue line, and a distinct red line. The pattern, number, and colors of these lines perfectly match the reference spectrum for Hydrogen.
Two lines are present in the violet/deep blue region on the far left of the spectrum, closely spaced.
One cyan-colored line is observed to the right of the violet/blue lines.
One prominent red line is present on the far right of the spectrum, indicating the longest wavelength line.
The characteristic pattern of two closely-spaced lines on the left, followed by a cyan line, and then a red line further to the right, matches the Balmer series.
